coredata.dc:title: Research on green synthesis and performance analysis of biomass-derived carbon quantum dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Hui
Zhan, WeiHui
Wan, MengJia
Bao, XinDe
Tang, Li
Guan, Xin
coredata.dc:description: Biomass-based carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have emerged as promising materials for sustain- able development due to their cost-effectiveness, ease of manufacture, and eco-friendliness. This study investigated the use of sawdust from various wood species—Pinus sylvestris var. mongolica Litv., Calocedrus macrolepis var. formosana, Cunninghamia lanceolata (Lamb.) Hook., and Zenia insignis Chun—as carbon sources for CQDs synthesis via a hydrothermal method. Using diverse analytical techniques such as transmission electron microscopy, X-ray diffractometer, ultraviolet-visible spectroscopy, fluorescence spectrophotometry, fourier transform infrared spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, differences in the properties of CQDs derived from different wood powders were examined. Distinct characteristics were observed among the CQDs obtained from different wood sources. CQDs from coniferous woods showed smaller particle sizes (2.5 nm to 3.0 nm) compared to those from broad-leaf woods (exceeding 15 nm). Unique lattice structures were exclusively found in CQDs from Pinus sylvestris var. mongolica, suggesting an amorphous structure in others. Variations in fluorescence intensity, carbon-to-oxygen ratios, and differing C-O bonding forms were also detected. These distinctions highlight the influence of wood type on CQDs properties, providing insights for tailored design and application. Moreover, this research underscores the potential for utilizing wood waste through CQDs synthesis, contributing to advancements in materials science and promoting sustainable resource utilization.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biomass-based CQDs
Wood waste
Hydrothermal method

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the preparation process of CQDs.

(a) TEM image and particle size distribution (upper right corner) of 1-CQDs; (b) TEM image and particle size distribution (upper right corner) of 2-CQDs; (c) TEM image and particle size distribution (upper right corner) of 3-CQDs; (d) TEM image and particle size distribution (upper right corner) of 4-CQDs.
Box plot of particle size of CQDs prepared from four types of wood flour.
HRTEM image of Pinus sylvestris wood powdered CQDs and enlarged lattice fringes (lower right corner).
XRD patterns of four kinds of CQDs.
Relationship between hemicellulose content in wood flour and CQDs particle size.
UV spectrum (red) and fluorescence spectrum (green and blue) of 1-CQDs (a), 2-CQDs (b), 3-CQDs (c) and 4-CQDs (d).
Photos of 1-CQDs, 2-CQDs, 3-CQDs and 4-CQDs ((a) under sunlight, (b) 254 nm UV light and (c) 365 nm UV light).
XPS full spectrum and fine spectra of C1s and O1s of 1-CQDs (a), 2-CQDs (b), 3-CQDs (c) and 4-CQDs (d).
FT-IR of four kinds of CQDs.
Median and mean particle sizes of four types of wood powder CQDs.
Content ratio of three major elements in wood flour of four types of wood.
Optical property data of four CQDs.
The peaks of four CQDs regarding the functional groups of C and O.
C, O, and N contents of four CQDs.

head.ce:title: Research on green synthesis and performance analysis of biomass-derived carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Wood Anatomy and Utilization, Research Institute of Wood Industry, Chinese Academy of Forestry, Beijing 100091, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Wood Anatomy and Utilization, Research Institute of Wood Industry, Chinese Academy of Forestry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Wood Anatomy and Utilization, Research Institute of Wood Industry, Chinese Academy of Forestry, Beijing 100091, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of Materials Engineering, Fujian Agriculture and Forestry University, Fuzhou, Fujian 350002, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Engineering, Fujian Agriculture and Forestry University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Biomass-based carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have emerged as promising materials for sustain- able development due to their cost-effectiveness, ease of manufacture, and eco-friendliness. This study investigated the use of sawdust from various wood species—Pinus sylvestris var. mongolica Litv., Calocedrus macrolepis var. formosana, Cunninghamia lanceolata (Lamb.) Hook., and Zenia insignis Chun—as carbon sources for CQDs synthesis via a hydrothermal method. Using diverse analytical techniques such as transmission electron microscopy, X-ray diffractometer, ultraviolet-visible spectroscopy, fluorescence spectrophotometry, fourier transform infrared spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, differences in the properties of CQDs derived from different wood powders were examined. Distinct characteristics were observed among the CQDs obtained from different wood sources. CQDs from coniferous woods showed smaller particle sizes (2.5 nm to 3.0 nm) compared to those from broad-leaf woods (exceeding 15 nm). Unique lattice structures were exclusively found in CQDs from Pinus sylvestris var. mongolica, suggesting an amorphous structure in others. Variations in fluorescence intensity, carbon-to-oxygen ratios, and differing C-O bonding forms were also detected. These distinctions highlight the influence of wood type on CQDs properties, providing insights for tailored design and application. Moreover, this research underscores the potential for utilizing wood waste through CQDs synthesis, contributing to advancements in materials science and promoting sustainable resource utilization.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as an emerging nanomaterial, were serendipitously discovered in 2004 as a byproduct of arc discharge synthesis of single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) (Xu et al., 2004). Since then, CQDs have gradually captured the attention of the scientific community. As a zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterial with excellent fluorescence properties, CQDs are typically composed of clustered carbon atoms, forming spherical or near- spherical structures with sizes ranging from a few nanometers to tens of nanometers (Jana et al., 2019; Çeşme et al., 2022; Liu et al., 2020b). Due to this dimensional limitation, CQDs exhibit distinct electrical, optical, and chemical properties compared to macroscopic- sized carbon materials. It possesses nano-scale dimensions similar to traditional semiconductor quantum dots, with excellent electron transport capabilities and outstanding fluorescence properties (Wang et al., 2015; Li et al., 2020). Furthermore, thanks to its rich composition of C, H, and O elements, it exhibits enhanced biocompatibility (Huang et al., 2021; Mindivan et al., 2024) and lower cytotoxicity (Mu et al., 2021), rendering it widely applicable in fields such as biosensors (Zhou et al., 2024; Caglayan et al., 2022), bioimaging (Ren et al., 2024), fluorescent probes (Dong et al., 2012), photocatalysis (Wang et al., 2017) and optoelectronics (Liu et al., 2015).

In recent years, the utilization of renewable natural resources, environmentally friendly solvents, and non-toxic chemicals for the synthesis of nanomaterials has become a crucial aspect of green synthesis strategies (Eskalen et al., 2024; Başkaya et al., 2024). Consequently, significant efforts have been directed towards developing green methods for synthesizing CQDs using natural biomass materials. Examples include grass (Liu et al., 2012), watermelon rinds (Zhou et al., 2012), banana juice (De and Karak, 2013) and strawberry juice (Huang et al., 2013). The preparation of biomass-based CQDs offers a new pathway for value addition and sustainable utilization of waste materials. Driven by this issue, the exploration of new carbon sources for the simple, economical, and environmentally friendly synthesis of CQDs remains highly important and desirable. Indeed, waste generated from wood processing undoubtedly represents a significant resource. As the world’s largest consumer of timber and the leading country in wood and wood product processing and trade, China annually generates substantial volumes of timber and wood processing output. According to the "China Forestry and Grassland Statistical Yearbook (2021)", in 2021, China’s timber production reached 115.89 million cubic meters. This consisted of 103.31 million cubic meters of log, 12.59 million cubic meters of firewood, 79.52 million cubic meters of sawn timber, 336.73 million cubic meters of wood-based panels, and 753.17 million cubic meters of wooden flooring. However, the comprehensive utilization rate of log in China was only 80 %. This leads to a significant waste of saw-dust, offcuts, and other waste generated during the logging, processing, and manufacturing processes. During the initial processing stage of converting wood into log, the generated debris is often used directly as fuel for burning or simply discarded, failing to achieve comprehensive utilization. Subsequently, during the processing of sawn timber and wood-based panels, a large amount of saw-dust and offcuts is also generated, which are similarly used for burning or directly discarded. In recent years, with the popularization of environmental protection concepts and the requirements of sustainable development, an increasing number of people have started to pay attention to the reprocessing of wood processing waste to improve the comprehensive utilization rate of wood. Through technological innovation and scientific management, these waste materials can be transformed into valuable products, such as biomass energy, wood pellets, particleboards and more (Bosch et al., 2022; Zawiślak et al., 2020; Li et al., 2023, Azambuja et al., 2018).However, there have been few reports on using wood waste as a carbon source for synthesizing CQDs. Therefore, exploring wood waste as a precursor for fluorescent nanomaterials holds the potential to increase the value of wood waste utilization. This approach not only facilitates waste utilization but also provides a new avenue for low-cost manufacturing of CQDs.

Based on this background, this study harnessed waste wood materials generated during the processing of both coniferous and broad-leaved woods as carbon sources to prepare biomass-based CQDs via a hydrothermal method. This experiment aims to prepare biomass-based CQDs from two types of wood: coniferous and broad-leaved. The study seeks to explore the differences in size, fluorescence intensity, and other aspects of CQDs prepared from these two types of wood, as well as the reasons behind these differences. The objective is to better understand the influence of wood type on the properties of CQDs, thereby providing scientific basis for the selection of suitable wood resources in the future. To provide a theoretical basis for further optimizing the characteristics of CQDs.

Using Pinus sylvestris var. mongolica, Calocedrus macrolepis var. formosana, Cunninghamia lanceolata, and Zenia insignis Chun (All are produced in Nanping City, Fujian Province, and provided by Sheng Sheng Wood Industry in Shunchang County, Fujian Province) as raw materials, the wood was ground and sieved through a 60-mesh sieve to obtain wood powder with a particle size of approximately 0.3 mm. Subsequently, the wood powder was placed in a drying oven at 60 ◦C for 3 hours to reduce moisture and minimize errors in weighing. Take 0.5 g of dried wood powder and add it to a beaker along with 20 ml of deionized water. Place the beaker on a magnetic stirrer (DF- 101S Heat-collecting Constant Temperature Magnetic Stirrer, produced by Shanghai Yujie Instrument) and stir at a speed of 500 r/min for 15 min. After stirring, transfer the mixture to a 50 ml polyvinyl chloride-lined hydrothermal reaction vessel (LC-KH-50 Polyvinyl Chloride-lined Hydrothermal Synthesis Reactor, produced by Shanghai Lichen Instrument). Place the reaction vessel into a preheated oven (Oven, produced by Shanghai Memelte) at 200 ◦C and allow the reaction to proceed for 8 hours. After the reaction is complete, remove the reaction vessel and cool it to room temperature. Then, filter the solution using a 0.22 μm filter membrane (0.22 μm Filter Membrane, produced by Jinan Kunkang Medical Equipment) to retain the solution. Afterward, the solution was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 minutes in a high-speed centrifuge, resulting in the CQDs solution. The CQDs prepared from Pinus sylvestris var. mongolica, Calocedrus macrolepis var. formosana, Cunninghamia lanceolata, and Zenia insignis Chun were respectively named as 1-CQDs, 2-CQDs, 3-CQDs and 4-CQDs. CQDs preparation process diagram is in Fig. 1.

The CQDs aqueous solution was placed in a centrifuge tube and sonicated for 10 minutes to ensure thorough dispersion of the sample. Subsequently, 10 μL of the above sample solution was dropped onto a thin copper support grid covered with ultra-thin carbon film and allowed to air dry naturally. The surface morphology and size distribution of the CQDs were characterized using a TECNAI G2 F20 transmission electron microscope (TEM) produced by FEI Company in the United States, with an acceleration voltage set to 200 kV.

The CQDs solution was freeze-dried to obtain a solid, which was then ground into a powder with a particle size of approximately 300 mesh. The crystal structure of the CQDs was analyzed using an Ultima IV X-ray diffractometer (XRD).

The CQDs solution was placed in a colorimetric dish with two transparent sides and two frosted sides, with deionized water chosen as the reference sample. The UV-3000 UV-Visible spectrophotometer produced by Shanghai Mepuda Instrument was used to analyze the UV–visible absorption properties of the CQDs, with a wavelength scanning range from 200 to 500 nm.

The CQDs solution was placed in a fluorescence-dedicated cuvette with four transparent sides. The F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer produced by Hitachi of the Japan was used to characterize the fluorescence spectrum curve of the CQDs. The excitation and emission wavelength ranges were set from 200 to 800 nm. The starting value of the emission wavelength was set at least 10 nm higher than the starting value of the excitation wavelength. The excitation and emission slit widths were both set to 5 nm.

The X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) produced by the American company VG was used to detect the surface chemical composition of materials. Using an aluminum-target X-ray source with a monochromator, a wide scan with a step size of 1.0 eV up to a pass energy of 200 eV, and a high-resolution region with a step size of 0.1 eV up to a pass energy of 50 eV, was employed to analyze the elemental content of the CQDs. The beam spot size was set to 400 mm2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of fourier transform infrared spectroscopy
ce:section.ce:para: The solid powder of CQDs was ground with potassium bromide powder in a 1:100 ratio, pressed into pellets, and analyzed using FT-IR on a Lambda 750S UV-Vis-NIR spectrophotometer to characterize the surface functional groups of the CQDs.

The quantum yield (QY) of CQDs was calculated using quinine sulfate (Dissolved in 0.05 mol/L sulfuric acid, with a quantum yield of 0.54) as a reference material. At the excitation wavelength of 360 nm, the quantum yield of CQDs was calculated as following formula (Watcharamongkol et al., 2024):
(1)
ϕ =
ϕ
r
⋅
I ⋅
A
r
⋅
n
2
I
r
⋅ A ⋅
n
r
2
•
Φ: Quantum yield of the sample
•
Φr: Quantum yield of the reference
•
I: Fluorescence intensity of the sample
•
Ir: Fluorescence intensity of the reference
•
A: Absorbance of the sample
•
Ar: Absorbance of the reference
•
n: Refractive index of the sample
•
nr: Refractive index of the reference

Microscopic morphology and particle size distribution of the four types of wood-based CQDs were investigated using TEM, and the results were shown in Fig. 2. In particular, the particle sizes of 2-CQDs and 4-CQDs were relatively large, so a 200 nm scale bar was used to facilitate the observation of the distribution of CQDs in the images. As shown in Fig. 2, at lower resolution, it could be clearly seen that all four types of CQDs were uniformly distributed in the images in a quasi-spherical shape. Using Image J software, the particle size distribution of four types of CQDs was plotted by randomly selecting 100 carbon dot particles, as shown in Fig. 2. The size range of 1-CQDs was between 1.340 nm and 4.321 nm, with an average particle size of 2.702 nm. The size range of 2-CQDs was between 10.051 nm and 22.161 nm, with an average particle size of 14.659 nm. The size range of 3-CQDs was between 2.174 nm and 4.861 nm, with an average particle size of 3.191 nm. The size range of 4-CQDs was between 14.301 nm and 30.358 nm, with an average particle size of 21.632 nm. In Fig. 3, box plots of the particle sizes of the four types of wood-based CQDs were drawn. Combined with the similarity between the average and median particle sizes in Table 1, it was found that the particle sizes from the four types of wood exhibit a certain degree of uniformity and consistency, which was also in agreement with the normal distribution results observed in the particle size distribution charts. However, at high resolution in Fig. 4, distinguishable lattice fringes were observed only on the surface of 1-CQD, with a calculated interplanar spacing of 0.2107 nm, which is similar to the lattice spacing of graphene quantum dots prepared by Wang et al. (2014b). Combined with the XRD analysis in Fig. 5, all four types of CQDs show broad diffraction peaks at 20.68°, which is also a characteristic diffraction of amorphous carbon, indicating that these CQDs are predominantly

amorphous (Eskalen et al., 2020). Meanwhile, 1-CQDs exhibit a prominent peak at 26.92°, corresponding to the (002) plane of graphene, suggesting that this carbon quantum dot contains stacked layers of graphene or graphite structure (Liu et al., 2023; Mindivan and Göktaş, 2023). Notably, no characteristic peak corresponding to the (100) plane was observed in the XRD of 1-CQDs, which may be due to the low crystallinity or weak in-plane structure. Based on the TEM and XRD results, it is confirmed that 1-CQDs contain both crystalline graphene regions and amorphous areas. Combined with the TEM and XRD results of the other three types of CQDs, it is confirmed that these CQDs are primarily amorphous, with the possibility that they may only locally exhibit crystalline structures.

According to reports by Liu et al. (2020c), the carbon core in CQDs generally consists of either crystalline or amorphous carbon structures. Due to this range of diversity from amorphous to crystalline phases, the issue becomes more complex. As reported by Wang et al. (2019), strong blue fluorescent CQDs were synthesized using gelatin through a hydrothermal method, which were also amorphous CQDs. Similarly, Zhao et al. (2020) reported the synthesis of CQDs using pine wood as a raw material through a hydrothermal carbonization method for the detection of Fe3+, where the CQDs were in a crystalline phase. In the book "Plant fiber chemistry", it is stated that the fiber arrangement in softwood is more regular, and the wood structure is more uniform, whereas in hardwood, due to the influence of vessels, the fiber arrangement is less regular. Therefore, there is a greater possibility of observing lattice fringes in CQDs prepared from the former. It was interesting to note that the

CQDs prepared from the four types of wood using the same method and process exhibit significant differences in particle size. This may be attributed to the book "Principles and Technology of Biomass Pyrolysis" that the decomposition temperature ranges of the three main components in wood were different: (1) The decomposition temperature range of cellulose is typically between 200 ℃ and 350 ℃. Within this temperature range, cellulose begins to undergo pyrolysis reactions, producing various low molecular weight products; (2) Hemicellulose exhibits strong reactivity, and its decomposition temperature range is relatively low, generally between 150 ℃ and 260 ℃. Within this temperature range, hemicellulose begins to undergo pyrolysis reactions, producing aldehydes, acids, ketones, and sugars; (3) Lignin has good thermal stability and generally begins to slowly decompose at around 200 ℃, undergoing hydrogen bond cleavage and aromatic ring destabilization, resulting in the production of phenols, aldehydes, ketones, and acids. Due to the experimental temperature set at 200 ℃, which was just at the decomposition temperature of cellulose and lignin, the pyrolysis of hemicellulose played a crucial role in the formation of CQDs. In the study conducted by Zhao et al. (2022) on the hydrothermal synthesis of lignocellulose-based CQDs from lignin and xylan, it was found that the structure of CQDs exhibits a "sunflower" morphology. Initially, the carbonization and decomposition of lignin provided nucleation sites for the growth of CQDs, followed by the gradual growth of small molecules derived from hemicellulose around the nucleus, ultimately forming CQDs. Therefore, it could be inferred that the content of hemicellulose has a significant influence on the particle size of CQDs. Combining the previous exploration experiment on the content of the three main components of the four types of wood, as shown in Table 2, a relationship between the particle size of CQDs and the hemicellulose content in wood was plotted in Fig. 6. It could be visually observed that there is a certain linear relationship between the two, which was consistent with previous literature reports.

The optical properties of CQDs are crucial evaluation criteria for fluorescent materials. The optical properties of the prepared CQDs are characterized through UV–visible absorption spectroscopy and PL (photoluminescence) measurements. The prepared CQDs aqueous solution exhibits a brownish-yellow color under visible light and shows no significant fluorescence under UV irradiation at 254 nm. However, under UV irradiation at 365 nm, it exhibits blue fluorescence (as depicted in the inset of Fig. 9(a)). As shown in Fig. 7, UV–visible absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra of the four types of CQDs are presented (detailed data can be found in Table 3). It can be observed that all four types of CQDs exhibit two distinct absorption peaks at 224 nm and 280 nm. The absorption peak at 224 nm is relatively weak, mainly attributed to π-π* transitions of CC bonds within the CQDs, which are largely independent of the atomic types forming these double bonds, indicating the presence of unsaturated compounds such as C-C bonds. On the other hand, the absorption peak at 280 nm is more intense, primarily attributed to n-π* transitions of CO bonds (Zhang et al., 2014), owing to the abundant hydroxyl groups in hemicellulose. The PL spectra in Fig. 7 show that under excitation at 376 nm, 1-CQDs exhibit maximum PL intensity with an optimal emission wavelength of 442 nm, while under excitation at 372 nm, 2-CQDs display maximum PL intensity with an optimal emission wavelength of 434 nm. Similarly, under excitation at 373 nm, 3-CQDs show maximum PL intensity with an optimal emission wavelength of 437 nm, and under excitation at 379 nm, 4-CQDs demonstrate maximum PL intensity with an optimal emission wavelength of 438 nm. This further confirms the blue fluorescence of the four types of CQDs. Notably, the QY of four CQDs was obtained in the aqueous solution to be 1.4 %, 0.4 %, 2.8 % and 2.5 % (quinine sulfate as the standard). As is well known, undoped CQDs typically exhibit low quantum yield due to the influence of surface defects and surface functional groups. The surfaces of undoped CQDs may have numerous defect states, which can capture excited-state electrons and holes. This process leads to non-radiative recombination, thereby reducing the luminescence efficiency (Liu et al., 2020a).

Interestingly, as shown in Fig. 8, the fluorescence intensity of the four types of CQDs increases with the excitation wavelength, reaching maximum fluorescence intensity at the optimal excitation wavelength. Subsequently, as the

excitation wavelength further increases, the fluorescence intensity decreases. This indicates that the four types of CQDs exhibit typical excitation-dependent emission behavior, which can be attributed to the unique properties of the CQDs (Liu et al., 2024). Additionally, a noticeable redshift phenomenon is observed in the PL emission. Some argue that this behavior is attributed to the presence of various surface functional groups on the CQDs surface, leading to multiple emission centers (Mao et al., 2014). Under different excitation wavelengths, a particular emission center may dominate. If the surface functional groups are diverse, the main emission center will change with the variation of excitation wavelength, exhibiting excitation dependence; conversely, if the functional groups are uniform, the emission centers will be homogeneous, showing excitation independence (Liu et al., 2009). Some researchers argue that the size

distribution of CQDs also affects their excitation-dependent fluorescence properties (Wang et al., 2010; Ray et al., 2009). Wang et al. (2014a) synthesized CQDs with excitation-dependent fluorescence properties ranging in size from 0.5 to 6 nm using hydroquinone as the carbon source and sulfuric acid as the catalyst. They suggested that the excitation-dependent fluorescence properties of CQDs are correlated with their heterogeneous size distribution. Mao et al. (2014) obtained CQDs with a size distribution ranging from 2.2 to 5.0 nm through one-step thermal decomposition of polyacrylic acid in glycerol. They suggested that the excitation-dependent fluorescence properties exhibited by CQDs might be related to their heterogeneous size distribution.

As shown in Fig. 10, the XPS full spectra of the four types of CQDs exhibit three prominent peaks at approximately 285 eV, 399.8 eV, and 532.4 eV, corresponding to the C1s, N1s, and O1s peaks, respectively. The elemental compositions of the four types of CQDs are presented in Table 4. In the C1s spectra of the four types of CQDs, three characteristic peaks were observed, corresponding to the C–C, C–O, and CO functional groups (Jia et al., 2012). The O1s spectra revealed two main peaks, O1s spectra revealed two main peaks, representing the C–O and CO functional groups (Lu et al., 2012). Detailed data can be found in Table 5. Although the full XPS spectra of the CQDs show a small presence of N, the specific types of nitrogen-containing groups remain unclear due to the lack of a detailed scan for N. Overall, the abundance of C, O, and N functional groups in CQDs is one of the key factors for their excellent water solubility, and also influences their fluorescence emission intensity.

FT-IR spectroscopy analysis, as shown in Fig. 11, reveals that all four types of CQDs exhibit a broad absorption peak around 3410 cm⁻¹ , which, in combination with XPS results, is likely attributed to the stretching vibrations of O-H and N-H groups (Zhou et al., 2024). A weaker peak around 2933 cm⁻¹ suggests the presence of C-H stretching, indicating alkyl group bonding. Additionally, the absorption at 1713 cm⁻¹ is likely due to CO stretching vibrations, pointing to the presence of carboxyl or carboxylate groups (Liu et al., 2020d; Mindivan and Göktaş, 2023). Peaks at 1612 cm⁻¹ , 1515 cm⁻¹ , and 1456 cm⁻¹ correspond to CC stretching, suggesting the presence of aromatic ring structures within the CQDs (Han et al., 2023). Stretching vibrations of C-N and C-O are observed at 1209 cm⁻¹ and 1099 cm⁻¹ , respectively, pointing to amine and ether linkages (Çeşme et al., 2022). These FT-IR results, combined with XPS analysis, offer a detailed characterization of the functional groups on the CQD surfaces, which play a crucial role in their chemical reactivity and fluorescence properties.

The oxygen content of CQDs is often linked to the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups, but this does not necessarily imply a higher degree of oxidation. The degree of oxidation can be assessed by the shift of the O1s peak towards lower binding energies (Wang et al., 2020). The greater the deviation, the higher the surface oxidation degree. As shown in Fig. 10 -CQDs exhibit the highest degree of O1s peak shift compared to 3-CQDs, indicating the highest degree of oxidation. Surface oxidation introduces defects, which can serve as trapping sites for excitation (Liu et al., 2018). The higher the degree of oxidation, the more defects on the surface of CQDs, resulting in a narrower bandgap and higher fluorescence intensity (Moon et al., 2014). Similarly, the lower fluorescence intensity of 4-CQDs compared to 2-CQDs is also due to this reason. However, despite the higher degree of surface oxidation in 1-CQDs compared to 4-CQDs, it exhibits lower fluorescence intensity. This may be attributed to differences in the raw materials used for preparation and variations in CQDs’ particle sizes rather than surface oxidation.

A hydrothermal method was utilized to synthesize CQDs using wood waste as the raw material. Through comprehensive analysis techniques including TEM, XRD, UV-Vis, FL, FT-IR and XPS, the structure and properties of the obtained CQDs were extensively investigated. By comparing the experimental results, the formation of CQDs was confirmed. TEM analysis revealed that the sizes of CQDs prepared from different wood waste were as follows: Pinus sylvestris var. mongolica (2.5 ± 0.5 nm), Calocedrus macrolepis var. formosana (14.6 ± 0.5 nm), Cunninghamia lanceolata (3.0 ± 0.5 nm), and Zenia insignis Chun (21.8 ± 0.5 nm). This provides a new pathway for the preparation of size-controlled CQDs using different sources of wood waste. Under 365 nm UV light, the resulting CQDs aqueous solution exhibited striking blue fluorescence, and fluorescence experiments confirmed their excitation-dependent fluorescence emission properties. This study also investigated the relationship between the content of the three major components of wood (cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin) and the size and fluorescence intensity of CQDs. Due to limited sample data, a detailed analysis of their specific correlation could not be conducted, but it can be confirmed that there is a certain correlation between them. This simple and efficient preparation method not only expands the utilization value of wood waste and enables sustainable utilization of wood, but also provides an important reference for the regulation and optimization of the performance of wood-based CQDs in the future. Furthermore, it offers the possibility to precisely control CQDs-related properties.

This study was supported by Fujian Provincial Natural Science Foundation Project (Grant #: 2024J01402]; Forestry Promotion Project of Fujian Province in China (Grant #: 2021TG13]; Science and Technology Innovation Fund of Fujian Agriculture and Forestry University (Grant #: KFB23146A].

Zhan Weihui: Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Wan Mengjia: Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Bao Xinde: Visualization, Validation. Xu Hui: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Tang Li: Formal analysis, Data curation. Guan Xin: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We extend our gratitude to all the students who provided the wood data for this study Our appreciation goes to Professor Jinguo Lin and Senior Experimentalist Xiaoyun Chen from Fujian Agriculture and Forestry University for his guidance.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fabrication of carbon dots-embedded luminescent transparent wood with ultraviolet blocking and thermal insulating capacities towards smart window application
